tool_calls.name: submit_final_result
tool_calls.args:
{"final_result":"# Interdependencies Among Space, Connectivity, and AI Segments — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

## Context

Effective Q1 2026, the CODM (Elon Musk) reorganized the post-merger consolidated entity (SpaceX + xAI + X Holdings, recast under common-control accounting) into three reportable segments: **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI**. The CODM evaluates each segment on **segment income (loss) from operations** (a GAAP measure), with no separate "Corporate/Unallocated" column at the operating level and no intersegment revenue line.

Below are two explicitly disclosed interdependencies and an assessment of how GAAP segment reporting treats each.

---

## Interdependency #1 — Internal Launch Capacity: Space → Connectivity (and, prospectively, AI), with No Intersegment Revenue

### What is disclosed

The S-1 explicitly states that the Space segment provides launch services to deploy Starlink satellites for the Connectivity segment, but **no intersegment revenue is recognized**. Instead, the costs of internal launches are **capitalized directly within the Connectivity segment** as part of "satellites" in Property, plant, and equipment, net, and depreciated over time. The company further states it "currently allocates a significant amount of its launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect[s] to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future" (e.g., for orbital data centers).

The S-1 itself acknowledges the distortion this creates:

> "Because the Space segment's revenue only reflects external customer launches—and not the massive internal demand from the Connectivity and AI segments—its revenue scale and growth are relatively lower compared to the rest of the company. The segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value and shared resources the Space segment provides to bolster the growth of the Connectivity and AI segments."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting obscures this relationship.** Specifically:

- **Space segment revenue is understated.** In Q1 2026, Space generated only $619M of revenue (~13% of consolidated revenue of $4,694M), despite being the launch provider for the Connectivity segment's ~9,600-satellite constellation. A market-priced internal launch transfer would materially increase Space revenue and reduce Connectivity's reported margin.
- **Connectivity's profitability is overstated on a standalone basis.** Connectivity reported $1,188M of Q1 2026 segment operating income — the only profitable segment — but this figure embeds launch capacity received at cost (capitalized, then depreciated) rather than at a market transfer price. The segment's apparent margin advantage is partly an artifact of the cost-capitalization convention, not pure operating economics.
- **No transfer-pricing or intersegment line item is presented.** The segment table contains no "intersegment" or "eliminations" row, so a reader cannot quantify the cross-subsidy from the financial statements alone.
- **The CODM measure (segment income from operations) is computed on this same basis**, meaning management's own resource-allocation metric inherits the distortion.

---

## Interdependency #2 — Starship and Terafab as Cross-Segment Enablers: Space → Connectivity and AI

### What is disclosed

The S-1 frames Starship not as a Space-only product but as multi-segment infrastructure:

> "Starship, a fully reusable transportation system that is designed to carry **crew, cargo, satellites, and data centers** to Earth orbit, the Moon, Mars, and beyond."

The risk factors make the cross-segment operational dependency explicit and bidirectional in terms of execution risk:

> "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale… our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites [Connectivity], V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity [Connectivity], and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure [AI]) would be materially and adversely affected."

A second linkage runs through Terafab (a Space-segment manufacturing capability):

> "We expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab's vertical integration. We believe this will provide us with a **critical competitive advantage in the race to scale AI infrastructure, especially as we begin our orbital AI compute satellite deployments**."

And at the enterprise level:

> "We believe our unparalleled launch capabilities represent the **foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business**."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting partially captures this relationship — but only on the cost side, and only qualitatively.**

- **Costs are captured, but in the "wrong" segment.** Starship R&D ($930M in Q1 2026; $3,004M in FY 2025) and Starship-related capex sit entirely in the Space segment, even though the disclosed purpose of Starship includes deploying Connectivity satellites and AI orbital data centers. The Space segment therefore bears the full R&D burden of a platform whose benefits accrue (in part) to other segments.
- **Strategic value to other segments is not quantified.** The risk-factor and Terafab language is qualitative; no dollar value of the "competitive advantage" conferred on AI or Connectivity is disclosed, and no allocation of Starship development cost to beneficiary segments is made.
- **Correlated execution risk is invisible in segment financials.** A Starship delay would simultaneously impair Connectivity's V3 satellite deployment, V2 satellite-to-mobile rollout, and AI's orbital compute roadmap. The segment tables present three apparently independent P&Ls and do not surface this shared single point of failure.
- **Shared talent, shared operational philosophy, and shared technology themes** (reusability, automation, vertical integration) are described at the enterprise level only; no headcount, overhead, or technology-licensing allocation across segments is disclosed.

---

## How These Interdependencies Affect Valuation and Segment Interpretation

### 1. Sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation is structurally unreliable

A conventional SOTP that values each segment on its standalone segment income would:
- **Undervalue Space.** Space's reported loss ($(662)M in Q1 2026; $(657)M in FY 2025) reflects the full cost of Starship and Terafab development, but excludes the economic value of internal launch services provided to Connectivity and (prospectively) AI. The S-1 itself flags that "the segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value" of Space.
- **Overvalue Connectivity's margin profile.** Connectivity's $4,423M FY 2025 operating income embeds launch capacity received at capitalized cost rather than market transfer price. A standalone Connectivity business would have to procure launches externally (or pay an internal transfer price), compressing margins.
- **Misstate AI's standalone economics.** AI's $7,723M Q1 2026 capex and $2,379M R&D are largely terrestrial today, but the disclosed long-term AI roadmap (orbital compute) is contingent on Starship, which is funded and reported in the Space segment. AI's growth optionality is therefore not self-contained.

### 2. The segments are not separable — the whole is not the sum of the parts

Because Space is the "foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business," and because internal launch capacity is allocated (not sold) to Connectivity and AI, the three segments function as a vertically integrated system rather than three independent businesses. A SOTP that treats them as separable would likely understate enterprise value, because it would not credit Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI.

### 3. Risk diversification is overstated by segment presentation

The segment tables suggest three diversified revenue streams (launch services, broadband, AI/X). But the disclosed Starship risk factor establishes a common operational dependency: a single technical failure or schedule slip in the Space segment would simultaneously impair Connectivity's satellite refresh and AI's orbital compute roadmap. Segment-level financials do not surface this correlated risk; an investor relying on segment diversification as a risk mitigant would overstate the diversification benefit.

### 4. Margin and capital-intensity comparisons across segments are not like-for-like

- Connectivity's apparent profitability advantage is partly a transfer-pricing artifact (Interdependency #1).
- AI's reported capex ($7,723M in Q1 2026) understates its true capital intensity once future orbital compute (dependent on Space-segment Starship/Terafab investment) is considered.
- Space's reported R&D intensity (R&D of $930M on revenue of $619M in Q1 2026 — a ratio above 150%) overstates the segment's standalone R&D burden because some of that spend benefits other segments.

### 5. CODM measure inherits the distortion

Because the CODM allocates resources based on segment income (loss) from operations — the same GAAP measure that obscures the internal launch transfer and concentrates Starship cost in Space — even management's internal capital-allocation signal is partially distorted. An external analyst using the same metric will replicate, rather than correct, the distortion.

---

## Bottom Line

| Interdependency | GAAP Treatment | Effect on Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **#1 — Internal launch capacity (Space → Connectivity/AI), no intersegment revenue; costs capitalized in Connectivity** | **Obscures** — no transfer price, no intersegment line, Space revenue understated, Connectivity margin overstated | SOTP undervalues Space and overvalues Connectivity margin; cross-subsidy not quantifiable from financials |
| **#2 — Starship/Terafab as cross-segment enabler (Space → Connectivity and AI)** | **Partially captures** — costs captured in Space segment; strategic value, correlated risk, and shared infrastructure not quantified | Space bears full R&D/capex burden of a multi-segment platform; correlated execution risk invisible; AI growth optionality contingent on Space-segment spend not visible in AI segment |

In both cases, the disclosed interdependencies mean that **the three segments are not independently valuable and should not be valued as separable businesses**. A valuation framework that credits Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI — and that adjusts Connectivity's margin for a market-based launch transfer price — would more faithfully reflect the economics than the as-reported segment financials.

---

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Segment Reporting Footnote, Risk Factors, Business Description, and MD&A"
    }
  ]
}"}

# Interdependencies Among Space, Connectivity, and AI Segments — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

## Context

Effective Q1 2026, the CODM (Elon Musk) reorganized the post-merger consolidated entity (SpaceX + xAI + X Holdings, recast under common-control accounting) into three reportable segments: **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI**. The CODM evaluates each segment on **segment income (loss) from operations** (a GAAP measure), with no separate "Corporate/Unallocated" column at the operating level and no intersegment revenue line.

Below are two explicitly disclosed interdependencies and an assessment of how GAAP segment reporting treats each.

---

## Interdependency #1 — Internal Launch Capacity: Space → Connectivity (and, prospectively, AI), with No Intersegment Revenue

### What is disclosed

The S-1 explicitly states that the Space segment provides launch services to deploy Starlink satellites for the Connectivity segment, but **no intersegment revenue is recognized**. Instead, the costs of internal launches are **capitalized directly within the Connectivity segment** as part of "satellites" in Property, plant, and equipment, net, and depreciated over time. The company further states it "currently allocates a significant amount of its launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect[s] to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future" (e.g., for orbital data centers).

The S-1 itself acknowledges the distortion this creates:

> "Because the Space segment's revenue only reflects external customer launches—and not the massive internal demand from the Connectivity and AI segments—its revenue scale and growth are relatively lower compared to the rest of the company. The segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value and shared resources the Space segment provides to bolster the growth of the Connectivity and AI segments."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting obscures this relationship.** Specifically:

- **Space segment revenue is understated.** In Q1 2026, Space generated only $619M of revenue (~13% of consolidated revenue of $4,694M), despite being the launch provider for the Connectivity segment's ~9,600-satellite constellation. A market-priced internal launch transfer would materially increase Space revenue and reduce Connectivity's reported margin.
- **Connectivity's profitability is overstated on a standalone basis.** Connectivity reported $1,188M of Q1 2026 segment operating income — the only profitable segment — but this figure embeds launch capacity received at cost (capitalized, then depreciated) rather than at a market transfer price. The segment's apparent margin advantage is partly an artifact of the cost-capitalization convention, not pure operating economics.
- **No transfer-pricing or intersegment line item is presented.** The segment table contains no "intersegment" or "eliminations" row, so a reader cannot quantify the cross-subsidy from the financial statements alone.
- **The CODM measure (segment income from operations) is computed on this same basis**, meaning management's own resource-allocation metric inherits the distortion.

---

## Interdependency #2 — Starship and Terafab as Cross-Segment Enablers: Space → Connectivity and AI

### What is disclosed

The S-1 frames Starship not as a Space-only product but as multi-segment infrastructure:

> "Starship, a fully reusable transportation system that is designed to carry **crew, cargo, satellites, and data centers** to Earth orbit, the Moon, Mars, and beyond."

The risk factors make the cross-segment operational dependency explicit and bidirectional in terms of execution risk:

> "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale… our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites [Connectivity], V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity [Connectivity], and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure [AI]) would be materially and adversely affected."

A second linkage runs through Terafab (a Space-segment manufacturing capability):

> "We expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab's vertical integration. We believe this will provide us with a **critical competitive advantage in the race to scale AI infrastructure, especially as we begin our orbital AI compute satellite deployments**."

And at the enterprise level:

> "We believe our unparalleled launch capabilities represent the **foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business**."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting partially captures this relationship — but only on the cost side, and only qualitatively.**

- **Costs are captured, but in the "wrong" segment.** Starship R&D ($930M in Q1 2026; $3,004M in FY 2025) and Starship-related capex sit entirely in the Space segment, even though the disclosed purpose of Starship includes deploying Connectivity satellites and AI orbital data centers. The Space segment therefore bears the full R&D burden of a platform whose benefits accrue (in part) to other segments.
- **Strategic value to other segments is not quantified.** The risk-factor and Terafab language is qualitative; no dollar value of the "competitive advantage" conferred on AI or Connectivity is disclosed, and no allocation of Starship development cost to beneficiary segments is made.
- **Correlated execution risk is invisible in segment financials.** A Starship delay would simultaneously impair Connectivity's V3 satellite deployment, V2 satellite-to-mobile rollout, and AI's orbital compute roadmap. The segment tables present three apparently independent P&Ls and do not surface this shared single point of failure.
- **Shared talent, shared operational philosophy, and shared technology themes** (reusability, automation, vertical integration) are described at the enterprise level only; no headcount, overhead, or technology-licensing allocation across segments is disclosed.

---

## How These Interdependencies Affect Valuation and Segment Interpretation

### 1. Sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation is structurally unreliable

A conventional SOTP that values each segment on its standalone segment income would:
- **Undervalue Space.** Space's reported loss ($(662)M in Q1 2026; $(657)M in FY 2025) reflects the full cost of Starship and Terafab development, but excludes the economic value of internal launch services provided to Connectivity and (prospectively) AI. The S-1 itself flags that "the segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value" of Space.
- **Overvalue Connectivity's margin profile.** Connectivity's $4,423M FY 2025 operating income embeds launch capacity received at capitalized cost rather than market transfer price. A standalone Connectivity business would have to procure launches externally (or pay an internal transfer price), compressing margins.
- **Misstate AI's standalone economics.** AI's $7,723M Q1 2026 capex and $2,379M R&D are largely terrestrial today, but the disclosed long-term AI roadmap (orbital compute) is contingent on Starship, which is funded and reported in the Space segment. AI's growth optionality is therefore not self-contained.

### 2. The segments are not separable — the whole is not the sum of the parts

Because Space is the "foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business," and because internal launch capacity is allocated (not sold) to Connectivity and AI, the three segments function as a vertically integrated system rather than three independent businesses. A SOTP that treats them as separable would likely understate enterprise value, because it would not credit Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI.

### 3. Risk diversification is overstated by segment presentation

The segment tables suggest three diversified revenue streams (launch services, broadband, AI/X). But the disclosed Starship risk factor establishes a common operational dependency: a single technical failure or schedule slip in the Space segment would simultaneously impair Connectivity's satellite refresh and AI's orbital compute roadmap. Segment-level financials do not surface this correlated risk; an investor relying on segment diversification as a risk mitigant would overstate the diversification benefit.

### 4. Margin and capital-intensity comparisons across segments are not like-for-like

- Connectivity's apparent profitability advantage is partly a transfer-pricing artifact (Interdependency #1).
- AI's reported capex ($7,723M in Q1 2026) understates its true capital intensity once future orbital compute (dependent on Space-segment Starship/Terafab investment) is considered.
- Space's reported R&D intensity (R&D of $930M on revenue of $619M in Q1 2026 — a ratio above 150%) overstates the segment's standalone R&D burden because some of that spend benefits other segments.

Because the CODM allocates resources based on segment income (loss) from operations — the same GAAP measure that obscures the internal launch transfer and concentrates Starship cost in Space — even management's internal capital-allocation signal is partially distorted. An external analyst using the same metric will replicate, rather than correct, the distortion.

---

## Bottom Line

| Interdependency | GAAP Treatment | Effect on Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **#1 — Internal launch capacity (Space → Connectivity/AI), no intersegment revenue; costs capitalized in Connectivity** | **Obscures** — no transfer price, no intersegment line, Space revenue understated, Connectivity margin overstated | SOTP undervalues Space and overvalues Connectivity margin; cross-subsidy not quantifiable from financials |
| **#2 — Starship/Terafab as cross-segment enabler (Space → Connectivity and AI)** | **Partially captures** — costs captured in Space segment; strategic value, correlated risk, and shared infrastructure not quantified | Space bears full R&D/capex burden of a multi-segment platform; correlated execution risk invisible; AI growth optionality contingent on Space-segment spend not visible in AI segment |

In both cases, the disclosed interdependencies mean that **the three segments are not independently valuable and should not be valued as separable businesses**. A valuation framework that credits Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI — and that adjusts Connectivity's margin for a market-based launch transfer price — would more faithfully reflect the economics than the as-reported segment financials.

## Context

- **Connectivity's profitability is overstated on a standalone basis.** Connectivity reported $1,188M of Q1 2026 segment operating income — the only profitable segment — but this figure embeds launch capacity received at cost (capitalized, then depreciated) rather than at a market transfer price. The segment's apparent margin advantage is partly an artifact of the cost-capitalization convention, not pure operating economics.
- **No transfer-pricing or intersegment line item is presented.** The segment table contains no "intersegment" or "eliminations" row, so a reader cannot quantify the cross-subsidy from the financial statements alone.
- **The CODM measure (segment income from operations) is computed on this same basis**, meaning management's own resource-allocation metric inherits the distortion.

---

## Interdependency #2 — Starship and Terafab as Cross-Segment Enablers: Space → Connectivity and AI

### What is disclosed

The S-1 frames Starship not as a Space-only product but as multi-segment infrastructure:

> "Starship, a fully reusable transportation system that is designed to carry **crew, cargo, satellites, and data centers** to Earth orbit, the Moon, Mars, and beyond."

The risk factors make the cross-segment operational dependency explicit and bidirectional in terms of execution risk:

> "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale… our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites [Connectivity], V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity [Connectivity], and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure [AI]) would be materially and adversely affected."

A second linkage runs through Terafab (a Space-segment manufacturing capability):

> "We expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab's vertical integration. We believe this will provide us with a **critical competitive advantage in the race to scale AI infrastructure, especially as we begin our orbital AI compute satellite deployments**."

And at the enterprise level:

> "We believe our unparalleled launch capabilities represent the **foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business**."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting partially captures this relationship — but only on the cost side, and only qualitatively.**

- **Costs are captured, but in the "wrong" segment.** Starship R&D ($930M in Q1 2026; $3,004M in FY 2025) and Starship-related capex sit entirely in the Space segment, even though the disclosed purpose of Starship includes deploying Connectivity satellites and AI orbital data centers. The Space segment therefore bears the full R&D burden of a platform whose benefits accrue (in part) to other segments.
- **Strategic value to other segments is not quantified.** The risk-factor and Terafab language is qualitative; no dollar value of the "competitive advantage" conferred on AI or Connectivity is disclosed, and no allocation of Starship development cost to beneficiary segments is made.
- **Correlated execution risk is invisible in segment financials.** A Starship delay would simultaneously impair Connectivity's V3 satellite deployment, V2 satellite-to-mobile rollout, and AI's orbital compute roadmap. The segment tables present three apparently independent P&Ls and do not surface this shared single point of failure.
- **Shared talent, shared operational philosophy, and shared technology themes** (reusability, automation, vertical integration) are described at the enterprise level only; no headcount, overhead, or technology-licensing allocation across segments is disclosed.

---

## How These Interdependencies Affect Valuation and Segment Interpretation

### 1. Sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation is structurally unreliable

A conventional SOTP that values each segment on its standalone segment income would:
- **Undervalue Space.** Space's reported loss ($(662)M in Q1 2026; $(657)M in FY 2025) reflects the full cost of Starship and Terafab development, but excludes the economic value of internal launch services provided to Connectivity and (prospectively) AI. The S-1 itself flags that "the segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value" of Space.
- **Overvalue Connectivity's margin profile.** Connectivity's $4,423M FY 2025 operating income embeds launch capacity received at capitalized cost rather than market transfer price. A standalone Connectivity business would have to procure launches externally (or pay an internal transfer price), compressing margins.
- **Misstate AI's standalone economics.** AI's $7,723M Q1 2026 capex and $2,379M R&D are largely terrestrial today, but the disclosed long-term AI roadmap (orbital compute) is contingent on Starship, which is funded and reported in the Space segment. AI's growth optionality is therefore not self-contained.

### 2. The segments are not separable — the whole is not the sum of the parts

Because Space is the "foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business," and because internal launch capacity is allocated (not sold) to Connectivity and AI, the three segments function as a vertically integrated system rather than three independent businesses. A SOTP that treats them as separable would likely understate enterprise value, because it would not credit Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI.

### 3. Risk diversification is overstated by segment presentation

The segment tables suggest three diversified revenue streams (launch services, broadband, AI/X). But the disclosed Starship risk factor establishes a common operational dependency: a single technical failure or schedule slip in the Space segment would simultaneously impair Connectivity's satellite refresh and AI's orbital compute roadmap. Segment-level financials do not surface this correlated risk; an investor relying on segment diversification as a risk mitigant would overstate the diversification benefit.

### 4. Margin and capital-intensity comparisons across segments are not like-for-like

- Connectivity's apparent profitability advantage is partly a transfer-pricing artifact (Interdependency #1).
- AI's reported capex ($7,723M in Q1 2026) understates its true capital intensity once future orbital compute (dependent on Space-segment Starship/Terafab investment) is considered.
- Space's reported R&D intensity (R&D of $930M on revenue of $619M in Q1 2026 — a ratio above 150%) overstates the segment's standalone R&D burden because some of that spend benefits other segments.

### 5. CODM measure inherits the distortion

Because the CODM allocates resources based on segment income (loss) from operations — the same GAAP measure that obscures the internal launch transfer and concentrates Starship cost in Space — even management's internal capital-allocation signal is partially distorted. An external analyst using the same metric will replicate, rather than correct, the distortion.

---

## Bottom Line

| Interdependency | GAAP Treatment | Effect on Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **#1 — Internal launch capacity (Space → Connectivity/AI), no intersegment revenue; costs capitalized in Connectivity** | **Obscures** — no transfer price, no intersegment line, Space revenue understated, Connectivity margin overstated | SOTP undervalues Space and overvalues Connectivity margin; cross-subsidy not quantifiable from financials |
| **#2 — Starship/Terafab as cross-segment enabler (Space → Connectivity and AI)** | **Partially captures** — costs captured in Space segment; strategic value, correlated risk, and shared infrastructure not quantified | Space bears full R&D/capex burden of a multi-segment platform; correlated execution risk invisible; AI growth optionality contingent on Space-segment spend not visible in AI segment |

In both cases, the disclosed interdependencies mean that **the three segments are not independently valuable and should not be valued as separable businesses**. A valuation framework that credits Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI — and that adjusts Connectivity's margin for a market-based launch transfer price — would more faithfully reflect the economics than the as-reported segment financials.

---

    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Segment Reporting Footnote, Risk Factors, Business Description, and MD&A"
    }

tool_output.output:
# Interdependencies Among Space, Connectivity, and AI Segments — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

## Context

Effective Q1 2026, the CODM (Elon Musk) reorganized the post-merger consolidated entity (SpaceX + xAI + X Holdings, recast under common-control accounting) into three reportable segments: **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI**. The CODM evaluates each segment on **segment income (loss) from operations** (a GAAP measure), with no separate "Corporate/Unallocated" column at the operating level and no intersegment revenue line.

Below are two explicitly disclosed interdependencies and an assessment of how GAAP segment reporting treats each.

---

## Interdependency #1 — Internal Launch Capacity: Space → Connectivity (and, prospectively, AI), with No Intersegment Revenue

### What is disclosed

The S-1 explicitly states that the Space segment provides launch services to deploy Starlink satellites for the Connectivity segment, but **no intersegment revenue is recognized**. Instead, the costs of internal launches are **capitalized directly within the Connectivity segment** as part of "satellites" in Property, plant, and equipment, net, and depreciated over time. The company further states it "currently allocates a significant amount of its launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect[s] to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future" (e.g., for orbital data centers).

The S-1 itself acknowledges the distortion this creates:

> "Because the Space segment's revenue only reflects external customer launches—and not the massive internal demand from the Connectivity and AI segments—its revenue scale and growth are relatively lower compared to the rest of the company. The segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value and shared resources the Space segment provides to bolster the growth of the Connectivity and AI segments."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting obscures this relationship.** Specifically:

- **Space segment revenue is understated.** In Q1 2026, Space generated only $619M of revenue (~13% of consolidated revenue of $4,694M), despite being the launch provider for the Connectivity segment's ~9,600-satellite constellation. A market-priced internal launch transfer would materially increase Space revenue and reduce Connectivity's reported margin.
- **Connectivity's profitability is overstated on a standalone basis.** Connectivity reported $1,188M of Q1 2026 segment operating income — the only profitable segment — but this figure embeds launch capacity received at cost (capitalized, then depreciated) rather than at a market transfer price. The segment's apparent margin advantage is partly an artifact of the cost-capitalization convention, not pure operating economics.
- **No transfer-pricing or intersegment line item is presented.** The segment table contains no "intersegment" or "eliminations" row, so a reader cannot quantify the cross-subsidy from the financial statements alone.
- **The CODM measure (segment income from operations) is computed on this same basis**, meaning management's own resource-allocation metric inherits the distortion.

---

## Interdependency #2 — Starship and Terafab as Cross-Segment Enablers: Space → Connectivity and AI

### What is disclosed

The S-1 frames Starship not as a Space-only product but as multi-segment infrastructure:

> "Starship, a fully reusable transportation system that is designed to carry **crew, cargo, satellites, and data centers** to Earth orbit, the Moon, Mars, and beyond."

The risk factors make the cross-segment operational dependency explicit and bidirectional in terms of execution risk:

> "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale… our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites [Connectivity], V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity [Connectivity], and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure [AI]) would be materially and adversely affected."

A second linkage runs through Terafab (a Space-segment manufacturing capability):

> "We expect significant speed and cost advantages from Terafab's vertical integration. We believe this will provide us with a **critical competitive advantage in the race to scale AI infrastructure, especially as we begin our orbital AI compute satellite deployments**."

And at the enterprise level:

> "We believe our unparalleled launch capabilities represent the **foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business**."

### Does GAAP segment reporting capture this?

**GAAP segment reporting partially captures this relationship — but only on the cost side, and only qualitatively.**

- **Costs are captured, but in the "wrong" segment.** Starship R&D ($930M in Q1 2026; $3,004M in FY 2025) and Starship-related capex sit entirely in the Space segment, even though the disclosed purpose of Starship includes deploying Connectivity satellites and AI orbital data centers. The Space segment therefore bears the full R&D burden of a platform whose benefits accrue (in part) to other segments.
- **Strategic value to other segments is not quantified.** The risk-factor and Terafab language is qualitative; no dollar value of the "competitive advantage" conferred on AI or Connectivity is disclosed, and no allocation of Starship development cost to beneficiary segments is made.
- **Correlated execution risk is invisible in segment financials.** A Starship delay would simultaneously impair Connectivity's V3 satellite deployment, V2 satellite-to-mobile rollout, and AI's orbital compute roadmap. The segment tables present three apparently independent P&Ls and do not surface this shared single point of failure.
- **Shared talent, shared operational philosophy, and shared technology themes** (reusability, automation, vertical integration) are described at the enterprise level only; no headcount, overhead, or technology-licensing allocation across segments is disclosed.

---

## How These Interdependencies Affect Valuation and Segment Interpretation

### 1. Sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation is structurally unreliable

A conventional SOTP that values each segment on its standalone segment income would:
- **Undervalue Space.** Space's reported loss ($(662)M in Q1 2026; $(657)M in FY 2025) reflects the full cost of Starship and Terafab development, but excludes the economic value of internal launch services provided to Connectivity and (prospectively) AI. The S-1 itself flags that "the segment financials do not reflect the foundational strategic value" of Space.
- **Overvalue Connectivity's margin profile.** Connectivity's $4,423M FY 2025 operating income embeds launch capacity received at capitalized cost rather than market transfer price. A standalone Connectivity business would have to procure launches externally (or pay an internal transfer price), compressing margins.
- **Misstate AI's standalone economics.** AI's $7,723M Q1 2026 capex and $2,379M R&D are largely terrestrial today, but the disclosed long-term AI roadmap (orbital compute) is contingent on Starship, which is funded and reported in the Space segment. AI's growth optionality is therefore not self-contained.

### 2. The segments are not separable — the whole is not the sum of the parts

Because Space is the "foundational competitive advantage that enables all other parts of our business," and because internal launch capacity is allocated (not sold) to Connectivity and AI, the three segments function as a vertically integrated system rather than three independent businesses. A SOTP that treats them as separable would likely understate enterprise value, because it would not credit Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI.

### 3. Risk diversification is overstated by segment presentation

The segment tables suggest three diversified revenue streams (launch services, broadband, AI/X). But the disclosed Starship risk factor establishes a common operational dependency: a single technical failure or schedule slip in the Space segment would simultaneously impair Connectivity's satellite refresh and AI's orbital compute roadmap. Segment-level financials do not surface this correlated risk; an investor relying on segment diversification as a risk mitigant would overstate the diversification benefit.

### 4. Margin and capital-intensity comparisons across segments are not like-for-like

- Connectivity's apparent profitability advantage is partly a transfer-pricing artifact (Interdependency #1).
- AI's reported capex ($7,723M in Q1 2026) understates its true capital intensity once future orbital compute (dependent on Space-segment Starship/Terafab investment) is considered.
- Space's reported R&D intensity (R&D of $930M on revenue of $619M in Q1 2026 — a ratio above 150%) overstates the segment's standalone R&D burden because some of that spend benefits other segments.

### 5. CODM measure inherits the distortion

Because the CODM allocates resources based on segment income (loss) from operations — the same GAAP measure that obscures the internal launch transfer and concentrates Starship cost in Space — even management's internal capital-allocation signal is partially distorted. An external analyst using the same metric will replicate, rather than correct, the distortion.

---

## Bottom Line

| Interdependency | GAAP Treatment | Effect on Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **#1 — Internal launch capacity (Space → Connectivity/AI), no intersegment revenue; costs capitalized in Connectivity** | **Obscures** — no transfer price, no intersegment line, Space revenue understated, Connectivity margin overstated | SOTP undervalues Space and overvalues Connectivity margin; cross-subsidy not quantifiable from financials |
| **#2 — Starship/Terafab as cross-segment enabler (Space → Connectivity and AI)** | **Partially captures** — costs captured in Space segment; strategic value, correlated risk, and shared infrastructure not quantified | Space bears full R&D/capex burden of a multi-segment platform; correlated execution risk invisible; AI growth optionality contingent on Space-segment spend not visible in AI segment |

In both cases, the disclosed interdependencies mean that **the three segments are not independently valuable and should not be valued as separable businesses**. A valuation framework that credits Space for the option value it creates in Connectivity and AI — and that adjusts Connectivity's margin for a market-based launch transfer price — would more faithfully reflect the economics than the as-reported segment financials.
